speaker: interviewer
text: Roll five. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to be looking at a few of the pictures that Mr. Szames has. Would you like to say a little bit more about your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I want to bring--

speaker: interviewer
text: --first few years in America? And then we'll be looking at these pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: The best years of being in America is basically since I was-- came to Columbus. I spent most of my time, as ridiculous as I see it, in 1953 during the Korean War. I was drafted. I got a letter from-- from-- greetings from-- from your president. And now you've got to understand, in 1953, I was not a citizen yet. And when I got that, I did not want to go fight. I did not want to carry a rifle or anything. However, I knew a lot of my friends, a lot of people from the old country, were drafted in those days. And I knew I had to go. So-- and I knew I will not go fight with weapons. So I went over to the post office, which used to be the recruiting-- on Town and Third, I think it was. The old post office-- and I talked to a recruiting sergeant. And he says, when you get that, you have not much choice. However, if you volunteer before your call-up date is concerned, you can volunteer in anything. And the only place I can guarantee you you will not have to fight is in the Air Force. So I joined the Air Force instead of going to-- to the army, because I didn't want to go in Korea. And then when I got there-- I guess America has its down things, too-- when I got in the army, I wasn't allowed to do anything, because I wasn't a citizen. And it handicapped me. But yet, as-- as silly as it can be, I could not work in a office where I had to do paperwork, but they put me out for guard duty to watch the F-86s, which in the '50s, it was the top secret, the 102s, which came out in 1955, I think. The F-102s, there's the-- the-- the fighters. They put me on guard duty to watch that, but I could-- I couldn't do much, because I wasn't a citizen. You had to have a clearance-- a-- a second clearance.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in 1955, when I worked in the public relations office, my commander came over. And he wanted-- he wanted something done or write or something. I said, I can't do it. He says, why? I say, well, because I'm not a citizen, and-- and since I'm not a citizen, I can't do it. I can't do this. He says, how did you get in here? So I told him I had to go on-- I still have the papers-- I had to get out what they call a Declaration of Intention. And I signed that, and I declared, and I became-- so that I could go in the service. But, you know, believe it or not, if I'd gone overseas and got wounded, I couldn't come back till they make me a citizen, which is what happened to some friends of mine. So anyhow, he gave the adjutant, he gave him 90 days to make me a citizen. So I went into New York, and my wife was supposed to have been-- at that time she was my girlfriend-- was supposed to have been the witness for me to become a citizen, but she was too young. So her mother was my witness. And I became a citizen in 1955.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when I-- when I came to America, I worked hard. And one of the best things that ever happened to me, I had one of the finest American-born girl, which my wife now for almost 40 years. And she gave me three wonderful kids. And that was the-- the best years. And I guess life continues. But my life is nothing but a bed of roses in America since-- since I married my wife and had the kids. And I'm going to show you some of those pictures, but I hope they show you some of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: How'd you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the early days, my wife was-- she wasn't even 13 yet when I first met her. She used to be-- she used to babysit next door to some friends of ours. And I remember one day I came home, and she was sitting on the porch right next door. And she was, I don't think she was 13 yet-- no, she was. And I went over and I started talking to her. And her mother-- her parents, they were divorced, but her mother used to be also a friend of the-- was going at that time with an ex-refugee, a newcomer to America. So she didn't mind going out with a greenhorn in those days. One of the few American girls, by the way, that actually did not mind to go out-- or her parents, her mother-- mind going out with a greenhorn. But the stigma of 'greenhorn' in the early '50s, late '50s was very big. For a boy like me, it was very hard to-- to go out with American-born girls that their parents allowed. A matter of fact, her side of the family, they-- they thought I was nothing but a greenhorn. They wouldn't, but thank god she had better sense. And we went down here for-- I was going with her for a couple of years. Then she moved to New York. And then when I went in the service, I got stationed in-- near West Point. I took my basic training at Sampson. Then I was stationed in Newburgh, New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I used to go in. That was only about 40 miles from New York, so I used to go into New York. And we picked our-- our [INAUDIBLE]. We got engaged, I think, when she was 16. Married her when she was just 17. Had our first kid when she wasn't even 18. And everybody said it wouldn't last, so-- so-- it's-- and that is life.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyhow, and if I may show you a few of those pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] questions?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that you would like to say before we go on to the pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there's a lot of things that I want to say, but I think we've been talking for quite a bit, and I don't want to bore. I can go into-- to different segments of-- of my life experience, but I've learned a long time that don't stare too much, otherwise you-- you take away the concentration of what you're trying to accomplish. The only thing I do want to say is-- and this is only my own philosophy of life since I was free-- I've said that to my rabbi, which is Rabbi Stavsky, I've always believed there had to be a purpose as to why I was saved and this guy got killed, or-- and my family. And I believe part of my purpose is to have-- like I have two boys, thank God, and my name will go on. Was for that reason. And the other reason is since I came to America and I tasted the freedom, I-- there's a few things that I swore with my life that I will never do is, one, is to be hungry again. And two, that I will always make sure that everybody knows that I'm a Jew, and I was never afraid. And when I was in the service, I flaunted that. I used that to the full extent, like getting out of work on Saturdays and details and whatever. I've always flaunted that. Hey, I'm a Jew. I don't work on Saturdays. Wherever I could, I could get away with it. Secondly is I've always worked on food since I-- since I can remember. I was always around food, from-- from Europe, volunteering in kitchens, till now. My livelihood is in a kosher market. Is-- and my kids, thank god, is never hungry since I can remember, unless they go on a diet. And I've-- I've maintained. I've maintained my-- my-- my goals pretty much were carried out from what I set out to do. And I think this was the purpose why I should be living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of-- from your experience, what-- if one message would be something appropriate to pass to your grandchildren and great-grandchildren, what would you want them to know about what you lived through?

speaker: interviewee
text: I still do. My kids grew up with-- with the-- with the knowledge that always remember where your foot-- where your background is all about. Remember your roots. Remember your zayde, or you remember your dad. Remember what he did. I've always used to talk for-- since the fifth to sixth grade. Like I said, I've always used to go around and spoke in the schools. And so it was-- I didn't even speak good English. And my opening lines used to be-- still is-- is, I don't speak good English. I can understand everything I say. And if you have trouble, that's your problem. If you don't ask any questions, you will not understand. But I understand every word I say. So kids used to laugh about it, but we went through it. I used to enjoy of talking to the school. And the message that I try to tell everybody is once, is not to forget the past. And should not-- should not happen. And in order to prevent it from happening, is not so much the Jewish kids themselves, although they should know, is the-- the-- the Gentiles, the schools. And that's why I used to speak to them, is if they know something, they see somebody. It's different when you talk to a group of young people and they see and they hear a person, than just reading it in a book. In a book, it becomes a story. And the message I tell them is remember, when you grow up, I've seen this old man that spoke to me. And he told me he was out there. So when you ever see hatred or bigotry, you can always relate, oh, this old man told me that this was happening in such-and-such a year. Someone went to a Nazi, and we went through a different period.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they have something to relate. And I think once-- once a generation goes by and doesn't remember a past, or can't relate to a past, then the future is doomed. And I've always-- I've always said that. And matter of fact, in most of my talks, when I do do them, I use that analogy to-- to let them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I also bring out how easy it is to-- to entice and divide and conquer by hatred, or pick on minorities. You'd be surprised when you go into a school how many people have never heard about a Jew and how easy it is to pick-- you take, for instance, certain parts of the towns or cities or states. You'll find it's only Protestant, or you'll find it's only Catholic. Or it's all black, and maybe there is two or three white kids in an all-black school. So all of a sudden, the blacks are the majority. And how easy it is to them to entice them to pick on the whites. Or you go in a white school, and you show them the difference. it's only two black kids, which they have, how easy it is. They're different. They're black. They stick out like a sore thumb, and all this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what I usually bring up, and when I start speaking those, I-- I-- one of the first things I say, now that you're all comfortable in this, I want you all look me all over. Look me over. What do I look like? Do I look like a Jew? And you'd be surprised the-- the-- the answers you get. And what I do, I said, now, so why was I killed? Why was I killed? I didn't kill anybody. I was too young. I was only six. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I-- I lead into, or they themselves will lead into, as to why. And it's a lot easier to tell them. So that's basically the message that I've had most of my life when I talk to. And I did that when I was in the service. I used to go out and speak to-- to the different groups. And I still go to churches.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to go on and talk about the--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: photos.

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture down--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead. You just point to it and talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This picture down here, this is my surviving. This is the surviving group that I was talking about. And here you'll find my zayde, my grandfather. And this is my mother. And this is one of the uncles, since my other uncle, Sam, he was in the partisans, and this was right after the war. He did not join us back yet. A matter of fact, he was not with us since he left, till America, before we met up. And here, this is me. And this is my cousin in Boston. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Francine. [INAUDIBLE], I would call her. And this is my brother Gerald. And this is my sister Fay. She's the oldest of us five. And there's my cousin Rita, and she was the youngest. And you're talking between those two maybe six month different in age. But this is the nucleus that survived. And we were constantly in-- because we were one family and very close, is the reason we survived. And basically he was the leader. It went one, two, three, four. This is the-- the-- the-- the group. And on this picture-- I don't know if you can see it or not-- but this is the picture when we first hit Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait one second. All right. Continue. This is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is the picture after we came to Germany, when we-- when we left Europe, Eastern Europe, Poland and all that traveling. We-- we joined this Hitlerjugend. We got landed, or they kept us in the Hitlerjugend camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- the uniforms that we-- the clothes that they gave us, issued us, was the Hitlerjugend uniforms. So we had to wear that as part of our everyday dress. And I'm wearing one of those formal Hitlerjugend.

speaker: interviewee
text: We did not wear their hats. We wore their shoes. We wore their-- their jackets, their shirts, and everything, but never our hat-- their hats. And I'm wearing the-- the formal. And he's wearing their-- their everyday, the informal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there's to of us. Who's--

speaker: interviewee
text: And this guys down here was our leader. The Madrich, is what we called us. He was the-- We Used to call him Madrich or Father or whatever, because we didn't have anybody. So that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose the other young boy in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a good-- a friend of mine. And this-- this young kid is a Ukrainian boy. He joined us-- or we met in the camp. And DP camps-- when we-- when we got out, you had kids from all over the world, all over from Eastern Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because basically when they organized us, when the-- when the Haganah was bringing kids, they skinned-- they skimmed Europe to find us. And that was that.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name was? Is?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Mordechai. Motel, in Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now it's on. This is? I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is the group what I called my gang. We were divided into different groups while they were training us for the-- for the Haganah thing. And if you notice this group down here, the ages, the oldest kid down here was, I think, was 13 or 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: But any one of those would be able to stand up against any soldier or anything that comes to him. If you notice, we all had the sticks. We all had those-- I was telling you about the clothes that we wore? And this was my gang.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right in here, if we look real close, I bet you you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Use this to point with--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right in here, in those pants, you would find there would be dynamite and hand grenades and everything is what we used to carry in here. And in those jackets, they used the button down here, and we always used to carry those in here. We never went in Germany. We never went in town without carrying that. But [INAUDIBLE] again, this guy was great with the stick fighting. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember the names of the kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I really don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm right over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember. But this-- this was-- it was a very close, close group. And this one down here was taken in France just before we went to the-- to the ship of the exodus. And this is one of the last pictures. We were in a kibbutz [INAUDIBLE], and this was our regular uniforms. And this-- I'm standing down here at the end of the-- the end of the line or the beginning. I was pretty much short, so it all depends how you look. And in order to make the camera, we sort of laying down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Squeezed you in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And that's me standing down here. But this was the-- the-- the last time that we were actually in a formation. And down here we were staying in a-- in a parade line. And they were telling us as to what to accept, what to do, what not to do, and everything else. And from then on-- this was the dusk, just about dusk, in the morning-- they loaded us on-- on trucks, and they brought us back over to the ship. And we boarded from-- from there. That was the last time we were on the ground. From there on, we were either in trucks or going up to the ships. And this was already-- if you look down here-- at least I've always maintained that-- I and my sister look more relaxed here than ever before. I don't have any tension, because we're going to America, and we're going to meet their parents. And this was in late '48. We already had our visas. We're still waiting for a quota. But we knew this was the last stop before coming from America. And this was in Preen, and this was a Dutch type of a resort place where they kept us. And there was some other kids and some other families that are waiting for quotas to get into America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, all those were displaced kids that are waiting-- orphans, so to speak-- and they're waiting to get in on a quota system. So my sister and I were staying on the-- on the-- what do you call them? Near the water. And from there-- from there, we used to go over to King Ludwig's palace, and this was on the dry side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some friends. Some friends of ours took that. And that's the last of the German. We have a lot more pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are looking at?

speaker: interviewee
text: You're looking at my official passport from the HIAS, which is the organization that my parents paid the money to bring us out to America. And they handled as displaced children trying to reunite-- I would imagine they worked with the-- with the Red Cross-- to unite children and parents. So this is their-- my passport, so to speak. It's one of the few papers that I had from them. And this is the official. And there, I have down here, if you can see that, with my picture. And this was taken in 1948, late '48, for a passport. And it gives just about where we came from, where we landed, and when.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it also has my Polish name, Izak, and my spelling and everyone-- there's a little story. S-Z-A-M-E-S. When I became a citizen in-- in New York City, the judge and the bailiff used to call on everybody. Before you were announced, you have time to change whatever you want to clarify. And the guy says to me, don't you want to change your spelling? I said, why? He says, it's-- it's different. I said, well, I am different. I said, I am different, that's why I'm in America.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't want to say, if it was good enough for my dad, it was good enough for me. And this is the way he spells it, and I want to spell it. So I spell it like this. And my brother who was already in America-- and when I came back-- and my sister also had their names changed. But I refused to change it. So my brothers changed it back. They had it S-H-A-M-E-S. And I refused to change it. The only thing I'd changed is my name Izak to Irvin. And it-- biggest part I wanted to change that for is because, one, Izak was more or less in the Polish, and I didn't want to have anything to do with that. And second, Irvin, they told me that Yitzhak and Irvin is a lot better than Izak. And I changed that. But I didn't want to have a Polish name to carry with me. So that's the only thing I changed. And it gives a little bit of a background of us. That's on that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I have-- OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This piece of paper, it shows when-- after we decided to-- to not go to-- to Palestine, and that means that you're-- you were allowed to become German citizens, or you were given some papers free of travel. And you had to renew them about every six months or every year. I think it was every six months. Because, one, we were still in DP camps, so we were still a ward of the DP camps. However, we had the-- the freedom of going all this. Part of the reason was, when you were in a DP camp, the German police couldn't do nothing to you. Even if you committed a crime, they had to turn you over to DP-- to DP. But if once-- a DP-- most of them, the DP person, were going to go to Israel, to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you had this kind of paper with you, you had the freedom of travel all over, but you became under the German jurisdiction. You didn't have much jurisdiction, or you didn't have too much protection from the DP. So this was one of those papers we-- which we had. This is?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the Declaration of Intentions, as I'd alluded before. When I had the-- the draft notice, and I didn't want to join the-- the-- go into the army or join the-- the fighting groups, and when I found out I have to-- I can volunteer and do-- and enlist into the Air Force, but before I do that I had to take out a Declaration of Intention. This has nothing to do with citizenship. I guess American paperwork is-- is funny. I had this certificate. With that, I could join the Air Force and I could do anything else, but it was-- inside the Air Force, there wasn't anything I could do. I could go overseas with it, but I couldn't come back to America with it, like a lot of our friends. And this is just strictly saying that I intend to become a citizen of the United States. And I intend to renounce all of my other countries. And, like I said, I was a pretty good looking fellow in that age.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pretty good looking. All right.
